text:
RESTAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS AND WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Gerald Hutchinson is a 70-year-old male with a history of high-risk locally advanced prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M0 at diagnosis), treated with androgen deprivation therapy, abiraterone acetate/prednisone, and definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (78 Gy to prostate/seminal vesicles with pelvic nodal coverage) approximately 3 years prior. He was confirmed castration-resistant on most recent lab evaluation, with PSA values rising from 0.3 to 2.3 ng/mL over 6 months in the setting of castrate testosterone (9 ng/dL). Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant identified on recent testing. Imaged today for restaging at time of CRPC declaration. ADT ongoing. Abiraterone recently discontinued.

---

SCAN TYPE:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
WHOLE-BODY Tc-99m MDP PLANAR BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT OF THE PELVIS

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules or masses identified. No pleural effusions. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Visualized osseous structures of the thorax demonstrate no sclerotic or lytic foci suspicious for metastatic involvement. Minor degenerative changes of the thoracic spine are noted.

CT ABDOMEN:
Liver is normal in size with homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions identified on this examination. Gallbladder is present without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct is not dilated. Pancreas enhances homogeneously without ductal dilatation or mass. Spleen is unremarkable in size and attenuation. Both adrenal glands are normal in morphology and size without nodular thickening. Kidneys enhance symmetrically with prompt excretion; no hydronephrosis, renal masses, or nephrolithiasis identified. No ascites. No free air.

Retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes: Multiple aortocaval and para-aortic lymph nodes are present, the largest measuring approximately 7 mm in short axis at the level of the renal hilum; these are not enlarged by size criteria and appear morphologically unremarkable. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the aortic bifurcation.

CT PELVIS:
Post-treatment changes are noted in the prostate bed and seminal vesicle region, with expected architectural distortion and subtle fibrotic stranding consistent with prior radiation and surgical/biopsy intervention. The prostate gland itself is markedly atrophic and irregular in contour, consistent with post-radiation fibrosis and the effects of ongoing androgen deprivation. No discrete soft tissue mass is identified within the prostate bed to suggest gross local recurrence.

Pelvic lymph nodes: Right iliac chain lymph nodes are noted, the largest in the right obturator region measuring approximately 8 mm in short axis (previously 18 mm on diagnostic MRI at time of initial staging; subsequently treated with elective nodal radiation). This represents a marked interval decrease. No new or enlarging pelvic lymph nodes are identified. Left iliac chain and bilateral inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

Bladder is well distended and shows no focal mucosal irregularity. No hydroureter.

Visualized osseous structures of the pelvis and proximal femora show no aggressive bony lesions. Bone density appears mildly reduced, consistent with prolonged androgen deprivation therapy-related osteopenia.

---

WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN:
Phase: Delayed planar whole-body images obtained at approximately 3 hours post-injection of Tc-99m MDP. Supplemental SPECT/CT of the pelvis obtained.

Upper and lower extremities: Normal radiotracer distribution throughout the appendicular skeleton. No focal areas of abnormally increased or decreased uptake in the long bones bilaterally.

Axial skeleton: Tracer distribution in the skull is symmetric and unremarkable. Cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine: Mildly increased uptake at several mid-thoracic and lumbar vertebral levels, which on correlation with CT correlates with degenerative endplate changes and facet arthropathy; no discrete focal lesion or cortical disruption to suggest metastatic involvement. Normal uptake pattern in the sacrum and sacroiliac joints bilaterally.

Pelvis: SPECT/CT of the pelvis demonstrates no focal abnormal radiotracer uptake within the iliac wings, acetabula, or pubic symphysis region. Mild symmetric uptake in the sacroiliac joints is consistent with degenerative change.

Ribs: Symmetric, unremarkable rib uptake bilaterally. No focal rib lesions.

No photopenic defects to suggest lytic replacement.

Overall: No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastatic disease. No new osseous lesions compared to the bone scan performed approximately 12 months prior.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No definitive evidence of distant metastatic disease on conventional CT imaging or whole-body bone scintigraphy at this time of biochemical castration resistance declaration.

2. Expected post-treatment changes in the prostate bed and seminal vesicle region following prior radiation therapy and prolonged androgen deprivation.

3. The previously enlarged right obturator/iliac chain lymph nodes have markedly decreased in size compared to initial staging imaging, now measuring 8 mm and consistent with treated disease; no new lymphadenopathy identified.

4. No hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral metastases.

5. Mild reduction in bone density throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton consistent with the chronic effects of androgen deprivation therapy; clinical correlation with DEXA scan recommended.

6. Note: Given rising PSA with rapid doubling time in the setting of castrate testosterone, conventional imaging may underestimate true disease burden. PSMA PET/CT has been requested and is pending, as this modality offers substantially greater sensitivity for detecting sites of metastatic involvement in the biochemically relapsed/CRPC setting.
